title: OpenAI floats 5% US government stake — and wants Anthropic, Google, and Meta to match
subtitle: The FT reports Sam Altman has pitched Washington a public-equity model, days after the same government forced OpenAI to delay GPT-5.6.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The Financial Times reported on July 2 that Sam Altman and other OpenAI executives have opened early-stage talks with the White House about handing the US federal government a 5% equity stake in OpenAI. At the company's recent $852 billion valuation, that stake would be worth roughly $42.6 billion. The proposal is broader than one company — Altman wants each leading US frontier lab, including Anthropic, Google, and Meta, to grant Washington a matching 5% through a sovereign-wealth-style vehicle. The FT stresses the talks are conceptual and would likely require an act of Congress.

The proposal lands one week after the Commerce Department forced OpenAI to delay the public launch of GPT-5.6, restricting the model to a small set of vetted partners while agencies reviewed its cybersecurity capabilities. Altman's public argument is that the American public deserves direct financial exposure to AI's upside — an Alaska Permanent Fund for frontier models. The unspoken argument is that a government that owns 5% of you is less likely to blacklist you, delay your launches, or blacklist your rivals in ways that reshuffle the industry overnight.

This is the second time in six months a frontier lab has proposed structural entanglement with the state. Anthropic's ongoing Pentagon dispute — and its recent restoration of Mythos 5 for select US organizations after the Commerce Department lifted export controls — showed how fast a lab's business can move when Washington acts. Public equity turns that dependence into a formal partnership. Critics will call it regulatory capture with a dividend. Supporters will call it the honest version of a relationship the labs already have.

A takeaway for learners: watch how proposals like this reframe the AI-safety debate. Ownership is a governance lever, and if it lands, the question of who decides what a frontier model can do shifts from a purely private choice to one where a public shareholder has a seat at the table. If you work on policy, product, or governance, this is the kind of structural change that quietly redraws the map — bigger than any single model release.
